title: Contracts
rules: separate read-only and mutating responsibilities when needed
procedure: Bind implementations later in bootstrap or DI.
Keep consumers dependent on contracts only.

primary_when: the request is specifically about interface design, service contracts, or system boundaries
the request is about extension points or decoupling consumers from implementations
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about runtime patching, direct UI layout, or metadata parsing

solves: how to define explicit runtime boundaries between consumers and implementations
how to expose extension points without leaking internals
how to keep dependencies pointed at capabilities rather than concrete types

core_principles: Contracts should expose capabilities, not implementation detail.
Boundaries should be as narrow as the real integration requires.
Read and write responsibilities should split when they change for different reasons.
constraints: Contracts must remain stable enough to support composition without speculative overgrowth.
Consumers should not need concrete implementation knowledge to use a capability.
Extension points must not become hidden service locator channels.
decision_rules: Use contracts as the owner when the main question is boundary design or capability exposure.
Split interfaces when mutating and read-only responsibilities evolve independently.
Reject abstractions that exist only to imitate layering without real runtime value.

preferred_patterns: read and write boundary separation where justified
avoid_patterns: contracts that mirror implementation classes one-to-one
service locator hidden behind broad interfaces

recipes.name: Define a stable consumer boundary
recipes.when: Use when multiple runtime systems need to collaborate without direct implementation coupling.
steps: Identify the capability the consumer actually needs.
Separate read and mutate responsibilities if they differ.
Bind the implementation later in bootstrap or DI.

pitfalls: Designing contracts that simply mirror concrete classes.
Adding interfaces for code style rather than boundary need.
Letting extension points expose internals accidentally.
review_checklist: Can consumers remain ignorant of implementation detail?
Were responsibilities split only where justified?
Did the design avoid speculative abstraction?

references.title: Local Reference: Framework Contracts
references.interpretation: Use this first for boundary design, capability slicing, and extension-point policy.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for runtime contract design.

references.title: Local Reference: Framework DI
references.interpretation: Use this to ensure contract choices remain compatible with explicit registration and lifetime policy.
references.why: Use to align contract shape with container and composition decisions.
